beats.narration_text: Halo — this is Liam, in for Bear. Today: bio, the skill that turns any real or fictional figure into a narrated cinematic cut. Alternating photoreal clips and Manim dark cards, audio measured first.

output: Phase 1: narration (Bear voice or character voice)
Phase 2: beat sheet — clip / card / clip / card
Phase 3: Higgsfield Soul-ID clips + world + legacy
Phase 4: Manim dark cards (title, quote, date, equation)

beats.narration_text: The skill has two visual tracks: Higgsfield clips — soul-id for the figure, world for their milieu, legacy for what they left — and Manim dark cards — title with name and dates, quote for a signature line, date for a pivotal year, equation for figures whose idea is a formula. Every bio answers three questions in order: who was this person, why did they matter, and what did they leave.

props.title: Two tracks. Three questions. One story.
files: SKILL.md — clip/card schema + soul-id density table
reference/style.md — dark documentary look (white on near-black)

beats.narration_text: Four phase-gated steps: gather facts and decide length from story richness; author the alternating beat sheet covering who, why, and impact — soul-id opens and closes; generate narration as the master clock; generate Higgsfield clips after the beat sheet is locked. The bio command authors and scaffolds. The clips command ingests Higgsfield output from a TMP folder. The build command renders the cut. Watch the placeholder cut — cards plus audio — before spending on footage.

props.title: Author → narrate → generate clips → render.

phases.label: AUTHOR
phases.note: gather facts — dates, quote, world, legacy; decide length from richness

phases.label: BEATS
phases.note: alternating clip/card sheet — soul-id open + close, who/why/impact

phases.label: NARRATE
phases.note: Kokoro or ElevenLabs TTS — actual_duration_s = master clock

phases.label: CLIPS
phases.note: Higgsfield soul-id + world + legacy; ingest from TMP/

phases.label: RENDER
phases.note: Manim dark cards + footage composite → assemble.py

props.footerNote: Watch the placeholder cut (audio + cards, no footage) before generating Higgsfield clips. Footage is expensive — approve the rhythm first.

beats.narration_text: Self-demo: Phase two beat sheet for Ada Lovelace, short length — five beats, alternating clip and card, soul-id opens and closes.

output: Phase 3 Higgsfield: paid — not run here

beats.narration_text: Five beats. Beat one: soul-id clip — Ada at the Analytical Engine, 1843; narrator: she was the first to see what a calculating machine could really do. Beat two: Manim title card — Ada Lovelace, 1815 to 1852. Beat three: world clip — Babbage's workshop, gears and punch cards; narrator: she translated Menabrea's notes and tripled them with her own. Beat four: Manim quote card — Note G, from her translation: the Analytical Engine weaves algebraical patterns just as the Jacquard-loom weaves flowers and leaves. Beat five: soul-id close — narrator: she published the first algorithm. One hundred and seventy years before the word programmer existed. Phase three Higgsfield clips are paid — the beat structure is the deliverable here.

props.title: BEATS — Ada Lovelace (short bio)
props.code:
B01 render: clip  clip_source: soul-id
  narrator: "She was the first to see what a calculating
    machine could really do."

B02 render: manim  card.kind: title
  name: ADA LOVELACE  dates: 1815 — 1852

B03 render: clip  clip_source: world
  scene: Babbage workshop, gears + punch cards
  narrator: "She translated Menabrea's notes
    and tripled them with her own."

B04 render: manim  card.kind: quote
  label: Note G, 1843
  text: "The Analytical Engine weaves algebraical
    patterns just as the Jacquard-loom weaves
    flowers and leaves."

B05 render: clip  clip_source: soul-id
  narrator: "She published the first algorithm.
    170 years before the word programmer existed."

Phase 3 Higgsfield: paid (not run here)

beats.narration_text: Mechanism one: the timing contract. Every beat's on-screen time — fade-out included — equals its narration length exactly. This is not a style choice. It is what lets the compositor drop footage on the right cuts. If you add animation time on top of a beat's duration budget, the cut drifts — the footage starts playing during the next narration line. The footage is B-roll; the narrator is not lip-synced to it. The narration is the timeline.

props.heading: Beat duration = narration length. Exactly.
props.body: Every beat's on-screen time — fade-out included — equals its narration length dur(bid) exactly. The scene budgets the hold to absorb the remainder. This is what lets composite_clips.py drop footage on the right cuts. Adding animation time on top of a beat's budget causes drift and shot overhang. The footage is silent B-roll; the narration runs over it continuously. The narration is the timeline.
props.quote: Every beat's on-screen time — fade-out included — equals its narration length dur(bid) exactly.

beats.narration_text: Mechanism two: the alternating clip-card structure. The bio enforces a clip-card-clip-card rhythm because the two tracks carry different kinds of information. The Higgsfield clips carry emotional and visual presence: who this person was, the world they moved through, what they left. The Manim dark cards carry precise symbolic content: dates, a verbatim quote, an equation. Photoreal is the star. Cards carry the dates and the words. Neither track does the other's job.

props.heading: Two tracks. Each carries what the other cannot.
props.body: Clips (soul-id / world / legacy) carry presence and atmosphere — who the person was, the world they moved through, what they left. Manim dark cards carry precision — dates, a verbatim quote, an equation. The alternating structure enforces this division. Soul-id always opens and closes; the figure stays the spine of the film even as the story moves through world and legacy beats.
props.quote: Photoreal is the star; cards carry the dates and the words.

beats.narration_text: Mechanism three: length is an output, not a target. The skill documents four length tiers — short, medium, long — but the target is not one of those tiers: it is the story's richness. A figure with one clean idea needs five beats. A layered life with several ideas, a turning point, and a multi-part legacy may need twenty. Compressing a beat below the consolidation floor to hit a clock does not make the bio shorter: it makes the viewer not register the idea. Never pad to reach a number, never compress to hit one.

props.heading: Story richness decides length. Not the clock.
props.body: The skill has four length tiers (short ~30–60s, medium ~1–2.5min, long ~2.5–5min) but the tier is a read of the story, not a target. A figure with one clean idea: 5–7 beats. A layered life: up to 24 beats. Soul-ID density scales with length — more beats means more soul-id anchors. Compressing a beat below the consolidation floor to hit a number makes it fail to register. Never pad; never compress.
props.quote: Do not write to a 30-second (or any) clock.

beats.narration_text: Bio: two visual tracks, one timing contract, and length derived from story richness. Soul-id opens and closes. Cards carry the dates. The narration is the timeline.

artifactLines: alternating clips + Manim dark cards — clip/card/clip/card
soul-id opens + closes every bio; world + legacy in between
beat duration = narration length exactly (no drift)
length = story richness; 4 tiers, none is a clock target

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Run bio on a real or fictional figure. Before you start: can you carry the who, why, and impact in narration alone — with footage as silent B-roll, not a speaking subject? And does Higgsfield have a Soul ID already trained for the figure, or do you need to train one first? If the soul-id does not exist yet, that is Phase zero — not Phase three.

props.runningText: can the story be told in narration over silent B-roll?
output: can who/why/impact be stated in narration alone?
does a Higgsfield Soul ID exist for this figure?
Phase 3: Higgsfield clips (paid) — watch placeholder first

beats.narration_text: Claude, Chronicling. I'm Liam, in for Bear.
